title: Family Background and Personal Life
summary: Hitler's family origins, personal relationships, and private life — documented from archival records and contemporary testimony.

sections.heading: Paternal Line: The Schicklgruber Origins
sections.text: Hitler's father, Alois, was born on 7 June 1837 in Strones, Lower Austria, and was registered as illegitimate under the name Alois Schicklgruber. His mother, Maria Schicklgruber, had worked as a cook in the household of a Jewish family named Frankenberger in Graz — a circumstance later exploited by wartime propagandists and contested by historians. The identity of Alois's father remains uncertain; the most credible candidate is Johann Georg Hiedler, whom Maria later married in 1842. In 1877, Alois formally changed his surname to Hitler (a variant of Hiedler), the name his son Adolf would carry to infamy.

sections.heading: Alois Hitler: Father
sections.text: Alois Hitler (1837–1903) was a customs official (Zollbeamter) who rose through the Austrian civil service to a respectable middle-class position. He was ambitious, authoritarian, and reputedly harsh with his children. He married three times: first to Anna Glasl-Hörer (d. 1883), then to Franziska (Fanni) Matzelsberger (d. 1884), and finally to his niece Klara Pölzl in 1885. Klara was twenty-three years younger than Alois. By his first two marriages he had two children: Alois Jr. and Angela. Alois Sr. died of a pleural haemorrhage on 3 January 1903 in Leonding while drinking his morning wine at the local inn. Adolf was thirteen years old.

sections.heading: Klara Pölzl: Mother
sections.text: Klara Hitler née Pölzl (1860–1907) was by all accounts a gentle, devoted woman who doted on Adolf, her only surviving child of five. (Of six children, three died in infancy; a fourth, Edmund, died aged six in 1900.) She had grown up as a ward in the Alois Hitler household and was his second cousin once removed; the couple required a Papal dispensation to marry. Klara developed breast cancer in 1906 and died on 21 December 1907. Hitler later described her death as the greatest loss of his life. A physician named Dr. Eduard Bloch — a Jewish doctor who treated her — was treated with rare personal courtesy by Hitler years later; he was permitted to emigrate to the United States in 1940 with his possessions intact. The case is one of the few recorded instances of personal gratitude overriding Hitler's antisemitism.

sections.heading: Siblings
sections.text: Adolf had one full sibling who survived to adulthood: Paula Hitler (1896–1960), six years his junior. Paula led an obscure life in Vienna, working as a secretary under the name Wolff to conceal her identity. She met with her brother only occasionally during the Third Reich years; Hitler provided her a pension but kept her at a distance. She never married and outlived the war by fifteen years, dying in Berchtesgaden in 1960. His half-sister Angela Raubal (née Hitler, 1883–1949), daughter of Alois by his second wife, was closer to Adolf during the Weimar years. Her daughter Geli Raubal lived in Hitler's Munich apartment and died there in September 1931 — a death officially ruled suicide by gunshot.

sections.heading: Geli Raubal: The Central Personal Tragedy
sections.text: Angela Maria (Geli) Raubal (1908–1931), Hitler's niece, moved to Munich in 1929 to study medicine. She lived in Hitler's apartment on Prinzregentenplatz. Contemporary witnesses and later historians suggest Hitler was obsessively attached to her; the precise nature of the relationship remains disputed. Geli died on 19 September 1931 from a gunshot wound to the chest, from Hitler's pistol, in his apartment. The police ruled her death a suicide; her mother accepted this verdict. Hitler was in Hamburg at the time of the shot. He returned immediately to Munich and was reportedly inconsolable. Geli's room at the Berghof was preserved as a shrine until the end of the war. Several biographers — including Ian Kershaw — consider her death the single event that most darkened Hitler's private psychology.

sections.heading: Eva Braun: Companion and Final Wife
sections.text: Eva Braun (1912–1945) was born in Munich and worked as a photographic assistant for Heinrich Hoffmann, Hitler's official photographer, where she met Hitler in 1929. Their relationship remained almost entirely hidden from the German public throughout the Third Reich years; she was kept from official functions and was largely unknown as his companion. She made two suicide attempts in the early years of their relationship, in 1932 and 1935. Eva lived at the Berghof retreat in Bavaria and was described by those who knew her as cheerful, athletic, and politically uncurious — interested in fashion, photography, and films. Hitler married Eva Braun on 29 April 1945, in a civil ceremony in the Führerbunker, less than 40 hours before both committed suicide. Eva Braun took cyanide on 30 April 1945; Hitler shot himself simultaneously. Their bodies were burned in the Reich Chancellery garden.

sections.heading: Nephews and Nieces in Britain and America
sections.text: William Patrick Hitler (1911–1987), son of Hitler's half-brother Alois Jr., was born in Liverpool, England. He emigrated to the United States in 1939 and after several attempts to leverage his family name for financial benefit, enlisted in the US Navy in 1944, serving until 1947. He petitioned Hitler — unsuccessfully — for a better position in Germany in the 1930s. He subsequently settled on Long Island under the name William Stuart-Houston. He had four sons, all of whom reportedly agreed not to have children, ending this branch of the family line.

sources.ref: Kershaw, Ian. Hitler: 1889–1936 Hubris. W.W. Norton, 1998. Chapters 1–2.
Hamann, Brigitte. Hitler's Vienna: A Dictator's Apprenticeship. Oxford University Press, 1999.
Bloch, Eduard. 'My Patient Hitler.' Collier's Weekly, 15 March 1941.
